content: Create a new address book and add the 20 contacts (by drag and drop), then make a filter with the rule From + is in my address book + <name of new book>. Add new contacts to the book by clicking the star in the message header, twice, to select the target book.

content:
When you reset your Windows PC, you delete your files.

So, even when you reinstall Thunderbird - the emails where on the PC/ OS you have reseted, when you was using POP3.

So maybe you or your email provider has a backup - that's the only way.

Or when you didn't used POP3, than your email are still on the server of your provider. Look on his Help-Page for a contact form or email-address. He'll need to know your email-address and maybe some further information, like customer number or else.

The alternative would be to complicate to explain here in simple words.

Hope I was helpful (and I'm sorry about your old emails)

content: TB menu > Account Settings > account > Copies & Folders > 'Place a copy in:' > change from Sent folder to other and point it to Inbox.

content:
Does clearing up disk space from this option(see image) delete the emails from server as well?

It depends on your account type - POP or IMAP?

I couldn't find the manual triggering of "Compact Folders".

Again, it depends on what you're trying to achieve. You can set a threshold in Settings, when compacting shall be triggered automatically. To compact a single folder, right-click the folder in the folder pane - Compact Folder. To compact all folders of an account, right-click the account in the folder pane - Compact All Folders
creator: christ1

content: Hi PD, this error is not related to the number of recipients in your email itself. Even when sending to a single recipient, the message “Policy-SCO1 You've reached your sending Limit” comes from your mail server, not from Mozilla Thunderbird. It usually means that your account has hit a temporary sending limit imposed by the email provider, for example due to too many messages sent in a short period, background or stuck messages being sent from the outbox, or security/rate-limiting protections triggered by the server. Sometimes hidden CC/BCC fields, mailing lists, or synced devices can also cause extra outgoing messages without it being obvious. To troubleshoot, check your Outbox and Sent folders for stuck or repeated emails, make sure no automatic CC/BCC rules or templates are adding recipients, and if possible try sending the same email via webmail to see if the issue also occurs there. If it does, the limitation is definitely server-side and not caused by Thunderbird. In that case, the best solution is usually to wait for the temporary limit to reset and contact your email provider to ask specifically about the “Policy-SCO1 sending limit” on your account.
